Author: Dinesh Raghupatruni
Title: 🧠 Continuous Learning for Agents: Breaking Down Memento
Text:
🧠 Continuous Learning for Agents: Breaking Down Memento
❓ The Problem (Why This Matters)
Most AI agents look smart at first, but over repeated use they act like they never learn from past experiences.
🗺️ Everyday example: Maps app that never learns
You drive home daily. On rainy days, one street floods and you always avoid it. Your GPS keeps routing you through the flood because it doesn’t remember yesterday’s detour. That is how many agents behave: they restart from scratch every time.
📚 Research agent example
You ask a research assistant: “Summarize the latest work on stochastic games for AI alignment.” It misses key citations and over-indexes on blogs. The next day, a similar query gets the same mistakes because it has no memory of your corrections.
🌍 Web search agent example
You plan travel with an AI search agent. Yesterday, it ignored train timetables in Paris. Today, you ask for Rome, and it again skips transport constraints. Unless we fine-tune the base LLM (costly and risky), the agent doesn’t adapt from past runs.
💡 The Big Idea
Memento gives agents episodic memory so they recall past tasks (both good and bad) and reuse them on new problems. Instead of re-training the LLM, Memento stores each past experience as a case (state, action, reward)
, retrieves relevant cases for the next task, and grows its case bank with each attempt.
This process is formalized as a Memory-based Markov Decision Process (MMDP).
- In a normal MDP, the agent decides actions based only on the current state.
- In an MMDP, the agent also has access to its memory of past cases, which influences how it chooses actions.
This mirrors human memory: we don’t rewire the brain every day; we recall relevant episodes and act with those in mind.
🔁 How Memento Works (Step by Step)
1) The Continuous Learning Loop
At each task, the agent retrieves a past case from memory, plans and acts with the LLM influenced by that case, observes a reward, and writes the new experience back into memory.
In this figure, the circles
s₀, s₁, …, sₜ
represent the states of the environment, whilea₀, a₁, …, aₜ
are the actions taken by the agent at each step. After every action, the agent receives a rewardr₀, r₁, …, rₜ
, reflecting how well the action worked in that situation.What makes Memento different is the addition of cases (
c₀, c₁, …, cₜ
) and memories (M₀, M₁, …, Mₜ
). The agent doesn’t just act in isolation; it retrieves relevant cases from past experience (blue arrows) and writes back new outcomes into its memory bank. This loop allows the agent to adapt over time. The red arrows show how the environment evolves naturally from one state to the next, while the blue arrows capture the agent’s behaviour of consulting and updating memory.In short, the figure captures the cycle: retrieve → act → reward → write → update memory.
🧪 Example — Research Assistant
Yesterday’s case: “summarize MARL papers → missed stochastic game theory.”
Today’s query: “alignment in multi-agent systems.” The planner retrieves yesterday’s case, sees the missing concept, and adds “search stochastic game theory + alignment.” The user confirms the summary is more complete. The new trajectory is stored for future queries. Over time, the assistant avoids repeating the same blind spots.
2) The Full Architecture
Memento alternates between two coordinated stages: Stage 1(Case-Based Planning) and Stage 2 (Tool-Based Execution). The overall design is shown in Figure 2 below.
🧭 Stage 1— Case-Based Planning (Planner).
On the left side of the figure is the Planner. A user query enters here. The Planner consults the Case Memory, which stores past experiences.
- In the non-parametric variant, it retrieves the K most similar cases.
- In the parametric variant, it uses the learned Q-function to score cases and sample the best ones.
The Planner then decomposes the query into smaller subtasks (shown in the middle box as Subtask 1, Subtask 2, etc.). These subtasks, along with expected results, are written into Subtask Memory for execution.
🛠️ Stage 2— Tool-Based Execution (Executor).
On the right side of the figure is the Executor. It takes subtasks from Subtask Memory and runs them using external tools connected via the Tool Register. These can include search engines, video analyzers, code runners, math solvers, or document parsers.
The results of each tool call are logged in Tool Memory (middle-right of the figure). Once execution is complete, the new experience (s, a, r)
is written back into Case Memory. If the parametric version is being used, the Q-function is updated online with this reward.
📗 Example — Web Search Agent
Suppose the user asks: “Plan Rome trip in 3 days.”
- Stage A (Planner): It retrieves the Paris case where metro schedules were ignored [Refer websearch agent example]. This time it ensures transport is included and decomposes the request into subtasks like “find attractions,” “get train timetables,” and “check museum hours.”
- Stage B (Executor): The Executor calls web search and timetable tools to complete these subtasks. Each result is logged in Tool Memory. After completion, the new Rome case is stored in Case Memory, ensuring that future city planning requests benefit from both the Paris and Rome cases.
📐 Making Sense of the Math (Step-by-Step)
This section unfolds the math
✅ Step 1: Define the Case Bank
The Case Bank is a notebook of past attempts.
Example: s = "Plan Paris trip"
, a = "Search museums + metro"
, r = 0.6
(user said incomplete). We store the case (s, a, r)
.
✅ Step 2: The Overall Policy (Breaking Down the Formula)
The agent’s decision rule (its policy) is written as:
π(a | s, M) = Σ_c [ μ(c | s, M) * p_LLM(a | s, c) ]
Let’s unpack each symbol:
π(a | s, M)
This is the final probability of the agent choosing action a, given the current state s and the memory M.
Think of it as: “What’s the chance the agent will do action a, considering today’s situation and what it remembers?”s: The current state or situation. Example: the user asks “Plan a Rome trip.”
M: The Case Bank (the episodic memory of all past cases). Example: yesterday’s Paris planning case.
Σ_c: The summation sign tells us we’re going to combine information from all possible retrieved cases c. Each case contributes to the decision.
μ(c | s, M): This is the probability of retrieving case c, given today’s state s and the whole memory M.
In other words: “Out of all the past cases, how likely is it that case c is the one we should trust here?”
These are the retrieval weights we compute in Step 3 using softmax.p_LLM(a | s, c): This is the LLM’s probability of choosing action a, when it is told about today’s state s and also reminded of case c.
Example: given today’s query (Rome trip) and reminded of Paris case, the LLM might output probabilities for actions like “include transport” or “skip transport.”So the whole formula says: To decide on action a, look at each case in memory.
Weight how relevant that case is (μ), ask the LLM what it would do if reminded of that case (p_LLM), and then add everything up (Σ).
The result is the final policy π, a probability distribution over actions that reflects both memory and reasoning.
✅ Step 3: How We Get the Retrieval Weights μ
We turn case scores into probabilities using a softmax.
Pick a scoring signal. Examples: similarity score, learned Q-value, or a hybrid.
Then compute:μ(c | s, M) = exp(score(c)/α) / Σ_{c'} exp(score(c')/α)
.
Tiny example with two cases (A and B).
Scores from similarity: score_A = 1.0
, score_B = 0.0
.
Exponentials with α=1: exp(1.0)=2.718
, exp(0.0)=1.0
.
Normalize: sum = 3.718
.
Weights: μ_A = 2.718/3.718 = 0.731
, μ_B = 1.0/3.718 = 0.269
.
Note on α: smaller α makes μ peakier (more confident), larger α makes it flatter (more exploratory).
✅ Step 4: Now Combine μ with the LLM’s Proposals to Get π (this is the missing link)
Assume the agent has two candidate actions today: a1 = "include transport"
and a2 = "skip transport"
.
Assume the LLM’s action distribution when conditioned on Case A:p_LLM(a1 | s, A) = 0.60
, p_LLM(a2 | s, A) = 0.40
.
Assume the LLM’s action distribution when conditioned on Case B:p_LLM(a1 | s, B) = 0.90
, p_LLM(a2 | s, B) = 0.10
.
Use the policy definition to mix them with the μ we just computed: π(a1 | s, M) = μ_A * p_LLM(a1 | s, A) + μ_B * p_LLM(a1 | s, B)
= 0.731 * 0.60 + 0.269 * 0.90
= 0.4386 + 0.2421 = 0.6807 ≈ 0.681
.
π(a2 | s, M) = μ_A * p_LLM(a2 | s, A) + μ_B * p_LLM(a2 | s, B)
= 0.731 * 0.40 + 0.269 * 0.10
= 0.2924 + 0.0269 = 0.3193 ≈ 0.319
.
Check: 0.681 + 0.319 = 1.000
(probabilities sum to 1).
Interpretation: because Case B strongly suggests “include transport,” and because μ still gives Case B noticeable weight, the final policy π leans to a1 = include transport
with ~68% probability.
✅ Step 5: Write Back (close the loop)
After executing a1
, suppose the user is very satisfied.
Record the new case: s = "Plan Rome trip"
, a = "include transport"
, r = 0.95
.
This is appended to memory so future retrieval can use it, and (in the parametric variant) the learned Q-function can be updated with this reward.
🧪 Results (Benchmarks)
The authors tested Memento on several benchmarks, each designed to stress a different kind of agent ability:
- GAIA (long-horizon reasoning).
GAIA is a benchmark of multi-step tasks that require reasoning across many hops. It evaluates how well an agent can plan, remember intermediate results, and still reach the final answer.
Result: Pass@3 → 87.88% (validation), 79.40% (test). - DeepResearcher (real-time web research).
This benchmark measures how well an agent can use external search tools to gather and integrate fresh information from the web. It is closer to how research assistants work in practice.
Result: 66.6% F1, 80.4% PM. - SimpleQA (factual QA).
A straightforward question-answering task where correctness depends on retrieving and applying the right knowledge quickly.
Result: 95.0%. - Out-of-Distribution (generalization).
Tests whether the agent can handle tasks that differ from training or past cases. This is a stress test of adaptability.
Result: +4.7 to +9.6 points improvement when using memory.
All of these improvements were achieved without fine-tuning the base LLM. The gains came purely from memory, retrieval, and the case-based policy — proving that Memento can continuously improve agents without retraining.
🚀 Why This Is a Game-Changer
Human-like improvement: recalls past experiences as episodes.
Low risk and cost: no fine-tuning, avoids catastrophic forgetting.
Effective: boosts performance across reasoning, live web research, and OOD generalization.
❓ Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)
Isn’t this just a Vector Database (Qdrant, ChromaDB, Pinecone) with memory?
Not exactly. A VectorDB retrieves items that look similar. Memento retrieves cases that are likely to be useful. Example: Case A is very similar but often failed; Case B is less similar but succeeded. A VectorDB tends to pick A; Memento learns to prefer B because it carries higher utility.
What if I put human labels (success/fail) into the VectorDB?
That helps, but retrieval remains mostly static. Memento treats retrieval as a policy with probabilities and exploration and updates it after each task using feedback. This is online adaptation, not just storage.
What problem does this truly solve?
The MESAS problem: Memory-Enabled Self-Adaptive Systems. Agents must adapt continuously while keeping the LLM frozen. VectorDB plus labels gives storage and look-up. Memento provides memory plus adaptive retrieval and learning from rewards.
🔚 TL;DR
Memento turns brittle, one-shot agents into continually improving learners. By combining case memory with a retrieval policy, it achieves steady improvement while keeping the LLM frozen — going beyond what a VectorDB alone can do.
